op.name: abs
op.notes: Element-wise absolute value; alias for absolute.
op.signature: we.abs(x, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text: ``np.abs`` is a shorthand for this function.

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

An ndarray containing the absolute value of
each element in `x`.  For complex input, ``a + ib``, the
absolute value is :math:`\sqrt{ a^2 + b^2 }`.
This is a scalar if `x` is a scalar.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> x = np.array([-1.2, 1.2])
>>> np.absolute(x)
array([ 1.2,  1.2])
>>> np.absolute(1.2 + 1j)
1.5620499351813308

>>> x = np.linspace(start=-10, stop=10, num=101)
>>> plt.plot(x, np.absolute(x))
>>> plt.show()

blocks.text: Plot the function over the complex plane:

blocks.code:
>>> xx = x + 1j * x[:, np.newaxis]
>>> plt.imshow(np.abs(xx), extent=[-10, 10, -10, 10], cmap='gray')
>>> plt.show()

blocks.text:
The `abs` function can be used as a shorthand for ``np.absolute`` on
ndarrays.
